seo.title: Instructor Guardrails for E-Commerce
seo.description: Compliance guide for Instructor agents in e-commerce. Meet FTC advertising guidelines, CCPA/GDPR requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: E-Commerce teams using Instructor for structured output extraction from LLMs face heightened compliance risks due to the need for accurate product claims, pricing, and policy representations under FTC, CCPA/GDPR, PCI DSS, and ADA regulations. Without guardrails, common Instructor failure modes like partial extractions or hallucinated enum values can lead to unsubstantiated claims or incorrect pricing, triggering customer disputes and regulatory enforcement. Vex provides essential verification layers to ensure Instructor outputs are reliable and compliant in high-stakes e-commerce environments.

complianceRequirements.description: Product claims must be substantiated and truthful; LLMs must not hallucinate benefits or features.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex verifies extracted product descriptions against schema enums and flags unsubstantiated claims before customer exposure.

complianceRequirements.description: All prices, discounts, and totals must match backend data without hallucination or partial extraction errors.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex retries and validates nested pricing objects in Instructor outputs to prevent revenue loss from incorrect pricing.

complianceRequirements.regulation: Return/refund policies must be faithfully represented
complianceRequirements.description: Policy details like timelines and conditions must be extracted completely without missing fields.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex ensures full nested object extraction for policy schemas, catching inconsistent depth issues.

complianceRequirements.description: Customer data handling and UI descriptions must comply with privacy and accessibility standards.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex validates PII fields and accessibility flags in structured outputs to enforce compliance.

industryRisks.name: Incorrect product pricing extraction
industryRisks.description: Instructor partial extraction leads to underquoted prices, causing refund disputes.
industryRisks.example: LLM extracts $49.99 as $39.99 due to hallucinated discount, resulting in mass refunds.

industryRisks.name: Hallucinated return policy terms
industryRisks.description: Model invents lenient refund windows outside schema enums.
industryRisks.example: Policy shows 'unlimited returns' instead of '30 days', violating FTC truth-in-advertising.

industryRisks.name: Missing accessibility fields
industryRisks.description: Nested ADA compliance objects fail extraction, exposing non-compliant listings.
industryRisks.example: Product schema misses 'screen reader compatible' flag, leading to ADA lawsuits.

industryRisks.name: Unsubstantiated product claims
industryRisks.description: Hallucinated enum values like 'FDA approved' for non-medical products.
industryRisks.example: Beauty product listed as 'clinically proven' without evidence, triggering FTC fines.

implementation.title: Wrapping Instructor with Vex for Product Extraction
implementation.description: Patch OpenAI client with Instructor and wrap with vex.verify() to validate product schemas including pricing and claims.
implementation.code:
from instructor import from_openai

from vex import verify

client = from_openai(OpenAI())

@verify
class Product(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: float
    claims: List[Literal['organic', 'vegan', 'cruelty-free']]
    return_policy: Dict[str, Any]

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    response_model=Product,
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Extract product details: Organic cotton shirt, $29.99, 30-day returns, vegan certified'}]
)

implementation.title: TypeScript Instructor + Vex for Checkout Validation
implementation.description: Use Instructor's Zod patching with Vex to verify checkout objects for PCI DSS compliance.

implementation.title: Nested Policy Extraction with Retry Guardrails
implementation.description: Handle Instructor's nested object failures by combining partial extraction retries with Vex verification.

caseStudyScenario.heading: Instructor in E-Commerce: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario:
FashionRetailCo deployed Instructor to extract structured product data from LLM-powered catalog enrichment at scale. Their Pydantic schemas included pricing, return policies, and FTC-regulated claims enums. However, during Black Friday traffic spikes, they encountered cascading failures: partial extractions dropped required accessibility fields (ADA violations), hallucinated 'clinically tested' claims on cosmetics triggered FTC complaints, and inconsistent nested pricing objects underquoted totals by 20%, causing $150K in refund disputes.

Without guardrails, Instructor's retry exhaustion left malformed outputs reaching production, damaging brand trust and triggering compliance audits across CCPA data handling and PCI payment flows.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: After integrating Vex.verify() wrappers around their Instructor client calls, FashionRetailCo achieved 99.9% structured output compliance. Vex caught and retried hallucinated enum values, enforced complete nested extractions for policies, and validated pricing against backend schemas before customer exposure. This prevented $2M+ in potential fines, reduced refund rates by 87%, and maintained ADA compliance across 50K+ product listings.

cta.heading: Comply with E-Commerce Regulations Using Vex
cta.description: Integrate Vex with Instructor today to eliminate compliance risks in your structured LLM outputs.
